food_subgroup: Chicken egg, whole raw (white + yolk, no shell), per 100g edible portion

ontology.foodex2_description: Hen egg, whole, raw, edible portion (shell excluded)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Singapore serving of eggs is 2 eggs. Size 2 eggs (60g total with shell) contain approximately 50g of edible portion (egg white + yolk). Two eggs (~100g edible) is the standard breakfast portion.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Egg (whole, raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F01060008; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F01060008 — Chicken Egg, whole, raw. Values are per 100g edible portion (white + yolk, shell excluded).

gi_source: GI not applicable — whole egg contains negligible carbohydrate (0.7g/100g) and is not classified as a carbohydrate-containing food. GI is not a meaningful measure for eggs.

hypertension.key_factor: The high cholesterol content of eggs (372mg/100g — an egg has ~186mg cholesterol) was previously a major concern. Current clinical guidelines (2020+) have substantially revised the egg-cholesterol-cardiovascular relationship, noting that dietary cholesterol has a smaller effect on blood cholesterol than saturated fat. Eggs are relatively low in saturated fat (3.1g/100g). The sodium content is moderate (142mg/100g edible).
hypertension.guidance: Current Singapore HPB guidelines suggest up to 1 egg daily is acceptable for most healthy adults including those with hypertension, with minimal cardiovascular risk. Patients with established dyslipidaemia or diabetes may benefit from monitoring egg intake (1 egg/day or 5–7 eggs/week maximum). The overall diet pattern (saturated fat, sodium, fibre) is more important than egg quantity alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Essentially no carbohydrate (0.7g/100g). High protein (12.6g/100g) and moderate fat that promote satiety. Current clinical evidence does not support limiting eggs for patients with type 2 diabetes at up to 1 egg per day. Eggs are among the most nutrient-dense low-carbohydrate foods.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Eggs are an excellent breakfast protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Replacing high-carbohydrate breakfasts (kaya toast + white bread, nasi lemak) with 2 eggs + vegetables dramatically improves morning blood glucose management. Prepare by boiling, poaching, or scrambling — avoid frying in excessive oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggs are the richest food source of biotin and the most bioavailable source of choline. High selenium (31.7mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.46mg), vitamin D (2.0mcg), and vitamin B12 (1.1mcg). Vitamin D is particularly relevant for RA patients who are commonly deficient.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggs are strongly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a nutrient-dense whole food. The vitamin D content (2.0mcg/100g), while modest, contributes to the 15–20mcg/day target important for RA patients. Choline in egg yolk supports cell membrane integrity. The pro-inflammatory concern of egg arachidonic acid is not clinically significant at normal intake levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The whole chicken egg (Gallus gallus domesticus) is arguably the most complete single food available — providing all essential amino acids in nearly perfect proportions (biological value ~100), along with 13 essential vitamins and minerals. The edible portion (white + yolk, no shell) of a 60g size-2 egg weighs approximately 50g. Raw egg white contains avidin, which blocks biotin absorption — cooking destroys avidin and makes all egg nutrients fully available. Very high in selenium (31.7mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.46mg), vitamin D (2.0mcg), and vitamin B12 (1.1mcg). High cholesterol (372mg/100g) is in the yolk — but dietary cholesterol has a much smaller effect on blood cholesterol than once believed. Used in virtually every Singapore dish category.
common_user_queries: how many eggs per day is healthy
is it safe to eat raw eggs

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F01060008).
notes: Values per 100g edible portion (white + yolk, shell excluded). A standard size-2 egg (60g total with shell) has approximately 50g edible portion. Raw egg white contains avidin which blocks biotin absorption — cooking is recommended. Salmonella risk from raw eggs is low in Singapore (pasteurised eggs available) but cooking is advised for high-risk individuals (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant). Egg is a major food allergen.
